sentences.text: Well, later on, when I went to Hebrew school, I learned to speak Hebrew.
That was--
We really didn't have any contact with them.
We saw them, but we didn't have any contact with them really, so-- but I really don't remember any anti-Semitism until the Hungarians occupied our area.
And then--
Yes.
Yes.
In 1938, we moved to another apartment.
We were there for about a year or two and then moved into our own house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was about-- only about two blocks away from where we lived before, so it wasn't really far.
The town, Uzhorod, was divided into two by the river.
So we stayed on the same side of the river that we were before.
And my father's business was not too far also.
So we actually-- I kept the same friends really.
Yes.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE]
No, because by that-- that-- there was only one gymnasium, so you either went to high school, which was-- if you didn't plan to go to college, you went to high school.
You planned to go to college, or university rather, then you went to a-- a gymnasium.
So there was only one high school, one gymnasium.
And then there was one teachers'-- they called it a lyceum, teachers' college.
And then there was a Hebrew gymnasium.
So those were [INAUDIBLE] the only options really.
So it wasn't-- the town wasn't so very large that it-- it would have more than one high school, or more than-- well, it had two gymnasiums, because one was a-- a Hebrew gymnasium.
The other one was a public school, Czech gymnasium.
I went to the Czech gymnasium right after elementary school.
And I went to five years elementary school.
And then I had to take an exam to get into the gymnasium.
And I went to gymnasium for eight years.
But actually, I went to the public school, the Czech gymnasium for four years.
And then the Hungarians occupied us and we couldn't go to the public school, so I transferred to the Hebrew gymnasium.
Well, in 1938, we still had radios and newspapers that we could get.
So we knew-- we were very well aware that war has broken out.

It was only later that we could not have any radios, couldn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't find out what's going on and later on, because newspapers were not-- I'm sure not reporting what was going on.
And there were a few people who had radios clandestinely, I mean secretly.
So I know that my father had a friend who had a shortwave radio.
And he used to go to his house.
And he would listen to radio and come home and tell us what's going on.
But other than that, you could not openly own radios then, Jewish people couldn't.
So-- and that's when anti-Semitism has begun.
In fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one time-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] excuse me-- [CRYING]
On Fridays, my father used to go down to the river, where the fisherman were, and buy fresh fish for Shabbat.
And one day, he went down to the river, and he saw one of the fishermen beating a young boy.
And my father said, why are you beating him?
And the fisherman got very-- and he said, well, you just stop it.
And my-- the fisherman got into an argument with Father, my father that it is none of his business.
And he reported it to the authorities that my father made slanderous remarks against Hungarians, about the Hungarian government.
And my father was arrested.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that was my first experience with anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I think it left my father very-- even after we had-- he had been released from jail, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- he was very worried and very afraid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess he realized that this-- this is the way things are going to be and was very worried about it.
I think this about 1939 or '40, maybe 1940.
Well, it certainly changed my father's life completely.
As far as we were concerned, we had to-- we couldn't go to public school.
Jewish people couldn't go to public school, so I went to the Hebrew gymnasium.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there were some other things also.

But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, as-- as young people, we didn't really pay attention to-- we-- all my friends were Jewish, so I guess I was not as exposed to the anti-Semitism as some other people were.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but while under-- before 1938, I had never heard the word Jew mentioned.
I mean-- and-- and, of course, we had religious freedom, so it's not like we didn't have the religious freedom and that's why I didn't hear the word Jew.
But certainly under the Hungarians, there was an awful lot of anti-Semitism.
And even when we were taken away from home, I think that the Hungarians very willingly and fully cooperated with the Germans.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We were-- yes, my father was-- we were able to go to synagogues.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That-- that we were able to.
With some, yeah, some.
Until April 1944.
Well, it certainly affected my brother.
My brother left in 1938 for Israel, because the war broke out and he was just at the age where he would be drafted for the army.
And he didn't want any part of the war, so he left illegally at that time to Israel.
And my parents were very, very upset, especially my father.
I remember him waking up at night walking the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] room, because it took my brother about three months to get to Israel.
And we didn't hear from him, so we didn't know what was going on.
And then finally, after the three months, he got to Israel and he was able to write to us.
But even when he got to Israel, they couldn't really land.
He went by ship, and they couldn't land [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Haifa or wherever it was-- they-- because it was under British mandate.
So they stopped-- the ship stopped further back and they had to wade out into-- to shore.
And he had a very rough time at the beginning there.
It affected my-- certainly my older sister Olga, because she got married in 1941.
And very shortly after that, her husband was taken into work camp.
At that time, a lot of the young man were taken into labor camp.
They were taken-- they would not take Jewish guys to the army, but they took them labor camps.
And he has never come back.
So a lot of them died in labor camp under the conditions they lived on.
So-- so it definitely affected her, because once he left, she has never heard-- we didn't hear from him.
And of course, he never came back.
No.
There was just no way.
We just assumed that he didn't-- didn't survive, because I'm sure that he would have come back if he had survived.
But he didn't.
I-- I think the mail was censored.
But we did get mail from my brother.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, yeah.
I-- I thought I would go into engineering.
And I was fully prepared in 1944, when I was graduating from gymnasium that I'll be going to university.
But of course, it never happened.

On the 24th of April, there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were placards all over town saying that all Jews will have to leave their homes, take with them whatever they can carry, and that's it, and that we have to report to a central point.
In our case, it was a lumber yard.
So-- my son's here.
So we took what we could with us.
And we reported to the lumber yard, where we stayed for-- it was April 24th until May 16th.
We were put up-- there were no buildings or anything.
So there were sheds [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where everybody-- I don't how many families in one shed.
No privacy.
No facilities really to speak of.
And that's where we stayed until May 16th.
None.
None whatsoever.
None whatsoever.
Actually, what happened was that in some towns in Slovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jews were taken away-- taken from home earlier.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we heard rumors that the Jews that were taken from home were taken to-- they had to dig their own pits and they were killed.
And-- but we wouldn't-- we didn't want to believe it.

The way we have found out about these things, that occasionally apparently when these people were shot down, there were children who survived by having other people fall on top of them so they-- they were not killed and that somehow they found their way backs.
And-- and they were the ones who told other people.
So they said, no, these children are imagining things.
It cannot be happening.
So as I say, we did hear.
But we didn't want to believe that anything like that is going on.
Couldn't imagine that it could be true.
Did everybody follow voluntarily when these placards were put up to go to the different places assigned?
Did anybody try to escape?
There was really no way to escape, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Hungarian people-- if-- if somebody did, the Hungarian people would report them to the German authorities.
There was no place to go to, because certainly Hungarian neighbors were not willing to help us, to hide anybody.
So there was just no way of doing it.
The Germans confiscated it from Jewish people.
In fact, my very good friend who I mentioned lives in West Orange now, Edith Alice, her grandfather owned the lumberyard.
Excuse me.
I have to blow my nose.
On May 16th, we were told again that we have to get all our belongings, and we have to go to the railroad station.
And we were all herded up.
And we were taken to the railroad station.
We didn't know where we were going.
And they put us into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the cattle cars, railroad cars without windows.
And hundreds of people in one train-- in one car actually.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took us three days until we arrived what we now know is Auschwitz.
We didn't know where we were when we arrived.
We just know that finally they opened the doors.
During the three days that we traveled, there was no food, except what we took with us from camp.
And we didn't have much then.
So some of the people even died in the cars.
They were not removed for the three days.
There were no-- there was no place to sit, so we're just standing there for the three days.
And after three days, we arrived in Auschwitz.
The doors were opened.
And we saw people in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some kind of clothing.
We didn't know what-- who they were.
But they were wearing gray and blue striped-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't call it uniforms, but clothes.
And apparently they were inmates from Auschwitz who were assigned to work, to have these people get out of the cars and line-- line them up.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they put men to one side and women went to the other side.

And then I remember my oldest sister saying that since my mother was with my younger brother, that she's going to go with my mother to help her take care of my brother, not knowing that that group was actually destined to go to the gas chamber.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
So I was with my sister Elizabeth in a different group.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the last thing I remember about my father was he came over to us and said-- apparently he-- he must have known where we were, because he said to us, to my sister and me, wherever you go, they ask you if you know how to do anything, always say yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think it stood me in good-- that was very good advice he gave me, because it helped me later on.
And that's the last time I saw him.
You're now at Auschwitz.
[INAUDIBLE] You were able to stay with your sister.
What was your life like?
What was-- what did you do there?
We were put into a barrack that had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wooden-- what do you call those?-- where we slept-- wooden [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like bunks.
And you have a lot of women sleeping together in these bunks.
In the morning, we would get a piece of bread and coffee.
And then in the afternoon, we would get a soup.
And that was it.
Very early in the morning-- oh.
How could I forget?
When we first came to Auschwitz, we were taken to a-- an area where-- we were marched to an area where they shaved our heads.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We're undressed and given gray dresses, no underwear, just a gray dress and wooden shoes.
Then we were taken back to these barracks.

On the way to the place where they shaved our heads and took our clothing, there was a big ditch, where apparently people from the train who couldn't-- who were too sick to move were put into these ditches with the dead, the living with the dead, just left there.
[CRYING] And later on, they took these bodies and piled them up high.
[CRYING] And they were just left there in a pile, because they had no time to bury them.
They had to take care of first other things.
Anyway, our heads were shaven.
We were given this gray dress.
And we were taken back to the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And every morning, about 4 o'clock, we went out to what was called a Zahlappell, they counted us, lined up and they counted us.
We stood there for several hours until somebody came and went through the whole camp counting.
Then we would go back into our barracks.
We didn't work.
We were not assigned any work.
But I remember I volunteered to go and pick up the coffee or bread, whatever there was, to another camp, because I always thought that maybe-- [CRYING] Excuse me.
Maybe I'll see my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I never did.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So there was-- I really didn't do anything in Auschwitz except standing in Zahlappell, morning and at night again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were in Auschwitz for six weeks.
And then, during one of the Zahlappells, they picked out-- during these Zahlappells, they used to pick out people who were not-- who got sick.
They were always picked out.
And then, after the six weeks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they told us that we'll be taken out of Auschwitz to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took us to-- back to a train again, to-- and we went to Gelsenkirchen, Gelsenberg in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And there, as we arrived, we were put into some kind of a big tent building.
We were given, I remember, a bucket of soup with no utensils, no nothing.
But somebody had a spoon.
And about 20 or so women had to share the soup, so everybody would have a spoonful.
And we would go around, because, God forbid, somebody would have more than the other.
That way, we made sure that everybody had their share.
And then, a day-- a few days later, some of the girls came down with typhoid fever or typhus.
I'm not really sure which one it was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- but there, we were taken to work.
And we worked at the railroad station, cleaning up debris from bombing that they had.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And here we finally had some kind of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] facility to shower.
It was outdoors.
But we had water, which we didn't have in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, in Auschwitz, even when you had to go to the bathroom-- to the latrine, not bathroom, latrine, we would-- we would be taken there.
We'd be allowed a certain amount of time, maybe five minutes.
And finished or not, you left.
And no toilet paper.
Nothing.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But in Gelsenkirchen, or Gelsenberg, at least we had something.
We had water.
And we had facilities.
And we were in Gelsenkirchen from the beginning of July.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I can't recall when it was, but one day some-- about four or five men came from Essen, Germany, from the Krupp ammunition factory.
And they picked out again women, that there were about 500 of us who they wanted to come and work for the ammunition factory in Essen.
And my sister and I-- and two of our friends who were with us all along came with us.
So we were taken to Essen then, to a camp on Humboldtstrasse.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There our accommodations, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had straw mattresses, I guess you could call them, straw inside of some kind of a cloth on the ground.
And that's-- that was our accommodation.
And there we went to work every morning.
We were woken up again for Zahlappell, for the counting.
And then we went to the train-- a streetcar actually, I think it was a streetcar-- and went to work in the factory there.
Some women worked at annealing ovens.
I myself, with a small group of women, worked outside again, cleaning up debris or bringing in raw material into the factory.
I remember in the winter, for instance, we were bringing in metal sheets.
Many times we would bring them in and we thought that our skin will come off with the sheets, because it was so cold.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we did that every day.
At night, go back to camp.
And then I think it was around December 12th for some reason-- I don't know why remember that.
But there was a bombing at the camp and our barracks were bombed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
